title: Agent Workbench
description: A desktop control plane for running multiple AI agents — Claude, Codex, Gemini, shell, and Python REPL — from a single window. PTY-backed terminals, voice input, and an operator panel that steers agents via tool calls.
challenge: Running several AI coding agents at once means juggling separate terminals, with no shared view of what each one is doing and constant context-switching between them. There was no single cockpit to spawn, watch, and steer multiple AI CLIs — and no way to direct them by voice while your hands are busy.
approach: Built on Electron and Next.js with a Node PTY sidecar, so every agent is a real terminal child process with full I/O — not a fragile API wrapper. An operator panel spawns, kills, and directs agents through tool calls, and push-to-talk voice routes straight to the operator from anywhere in the app. State persists locally at ~/.agent-workbench; nothing leaves the machine beyond the AI APIs you're already signed in to.

metrics.label: Concurrent agent types (Claude, Codex, Gemini, shell, Python)
State and logs persist at ~/.agent-workbench — no cloud
Push-to-talk operator panel — hold Space anywhere to dictate

outcomes: Multi-agent terminal control plane with shared operator visibility.
Voice-to-agent dispatch from any context in the app.
Session persistence — exited agents stay visible for output review.

title: Polymarket Trading Bot
description: A modular prediction market signal pipeline for Polymarket US sports markets. Phases 0–2 active: ingestion, normalization, and a model engine that scores edge, liquidity, timing, and position sizing. Execution gated by design.
challenge: Prediction market trading requires combining sportsbook odds, CLOB depth, timing signals, and risk state into a single coherent model — and doing it in a way where the signal logic is auditable and the execution step is safely gated until validated.
approach: Four-phase Docker pipeline: risk bridge → ingestion → model/decision engine → execution (disabled). The core model engine is pure Python stdlib — no external ML dependencies. Phase 2 emits decision packets with execution_eligible: false. All secrets and account data are gitignored; the engine operates on normalized data only.

metrics.label: Unit tests passing across contract, ingestion, model, and risk
External dependencies in core model engine (pure stdlib)
Phase 3 execution disabled by default — validate first

outcomes: Full signal pipeline from market discovery to sized order intents.
Auditable model: every signal attribution logged per decision packet.
Paper-trade-first design: inspect output for days before considering live use.

title: Navore Market
description: A San Diego food marketplace connecting local producers with consumers through a modern, mobile-first operating model. USDA-backed and built around real logistics, trust, and neighborhood demand.
challenge: Local food systems are fragmented across producer discovery, ordering, logistics, trust, and recurring neighborhood demand. The opportunity required more than a storefront: it needed a practical operating model.
approach: I help build Navore as a marketplace and operating model together—pairing customer experience with producer relationships, hub operations, grant strategy, logistics, and feedback loops.

metrics.label: USDA-backed funding
Initial operating market
Marketplace and hub operations

outcomes: Launched a real local-food marketplace and neighborhood hub (La Jolla, Jan 2026).
USDA-backed funding ($450k+) supporting expansion of the model.
Created operating workflows connecting demand, producers, fulfillment, and growth.

title: Personal AI Operations System
description: A private, local-first AI operations system for agent workflows: gateway, memory RAG, Discord collaboration, dashboard visibility, scheduled briefs, and multi-machine orchestration.
challenge: Most AI assistants start cold every session, can't run unattended, and break the moment two of them touch the same work. To be genuinely useful, agents need memory that compounds over time, a way to coordinate and govern their own work safely, live visibility into what's running, and the ability to operate across machines and channels — not just a chat window.
approach: I architected a local-first agent operating system with a deliberate division of labor: one high-judgment orchestrating model plans and applies, while specialized local Ollama lanes (running qwen3-coder:30b for generation, research, review, and summarization) absorb the heavy token volume at zero marginal cost. Durable state is the backbone — a retrieval-augmented memory layer of working context, daily logs, and semantic recall lets the system compound knowledge instead of starting cold each session. Self-governance is enforced in code: a single-source task store projects to human-readable boards, state advances along a forward-only DAG with audited escape hatches, and Kleppmann-style leases plus monotonic fencing tokens let multiple sessions and two machines operate the same repository concurrently without stale-writer corruption. A FastAPI dashboard, status caches, and scheduled heartbeat checks provide always-on observability, and a provider-neutral driver layer lets different agent CLIs drive the same core through thin adapters behind an automated parity check.

metrics.label: Specialized local-model lanes (generation, research, review, governance), steered by one orchestrating model
Machines coordinated — an always-on host plus an on-demand peer sharing one model host
Private Ollama inference; memory and state never leave the machines
Durable cross-session memory — working context, daily logs, semantic recall
Kleppmann-style fencing tokens for safe multi-session concurrency
Unified ops dashboard, status caches, and heartbeat health checks

outcomes: Memory that compounds across sessions — the system improves with use instead of starting cold.
Safe parallel operation: leases and fencing tokens let multiple agent sessions share one repo without conflicts.
A provider-neutral core — different AI coding tools all drive one shared task store, memory, and governance layer.
Automated recurring briefs, health checks, and a disciplined start / checkpoint / end session lifecycle.

title: HealthRise EHR & Revenue Operations
description: A healthtech startup combining electronic health record software, revenue cycle management, and consulting services to improve healthcare operations.
challenge: Healthcare organizations often manage disconnected clinical, billing, and operational workflows. HealthRise addressed the need for technology and hands-on operational support within one startup offering.
approach: As Technical Program Manager I drove ML/AI integration across global delivery teams, alongside strategic planning and financial forecasting — connecting the technology layer to the operational realities of healthcare delivery and reimbursement.

metrics.label: Clinical workflow technology
Revenue cycle management
Operational advisory services

outcomes: Drove ML/AI integration across global delivery teams.
Led strategic planning and financial forecasting for the program portfolio.
Connected clinical workflows with revenue cycle operations.

title: Globalization Photojournalism Exhibit
description: A photojournalism exhibit exploring globalization through fieldwork in Tibet and Mongolia.
challenge: Globalization is often discussed through abstract economic and political language. The project sought to document how it appeared in lived environments and everyday experience.
approach: I conducted fieldwork in Tibet and Mongolia, using photography and firsthand observation to build a visual narrative around globalization and cultural change.

metrics.label: Fieldwork regions
Final public format
Research and storytelling method

outcomes: Produced a photojournalism exhibit based on international fieldwork.
Translated an abstract global issue into a visual narrative.
Documented perspectives and environments across Tibet and Mongolia.
